column4: A deconstructivist explosion of glass and aluminum that shards through the museum's original stone walls.
column7: The design was famously first sketched by Libeskind on a napkin while dining at the ROM.
column8: A dramatic study in 'structural tension'; the way the aluminum and glass 'shards' intersect the original 1914 stone facade is a masterclass in architectural contrast.

column4: A high-performance modernization of a prominent Yonge and Bloor intersection corner featuring vertical glass fins.
column7: The renovation added a 'shimmer' to the intersection using high-performance glass fins that change appearance based on the viewer's angle.
column8: A study in 'Adaptive Re-skinning.' The project replaced a dated facade with a curtain wall that adds a dynamic, contemporary layer to the Mink Mile.
column9: The shimmering glass corner at Yonge and Bloor.

column4: The global 'Gold Standard' of the International Style; a cluster of six black-painted steel and glass towers.
column7: Mies designed specific window blinds for the entire complex that only have three positions—open, closed, or halfway—to maintain a uniform facade.
column8: 'Less is More' in physical form. The external I-beam mullions guide the eye upward, serving as a masterpiece of geometric precision and material honesty.
column9: Look for the black steel towers with the cows in the grass nearby.

column4: A prismatic, glass-skinned building that serves as a digital-era gateway and student gathering hub on Yonge.
column7: The building has no 'back'; all sides are covered in a complex glass skin with a digital frit pattern to control light.
column8: The faceted blue ceiling in the lobby mimics a 'gathering space under a bridge,' creating a vibrant urban entry point.

column4: The final home of William Lyon Mackenzie, Toronto’s first mayor. A rare surviving mid-19th-century Georgian row house.
column7: Known as one of Toronto's most haunted sites; the printing press in the basement is said to click on its own at night.
column8: A study in Architectural Persistence. The flat Georgian brickwork stands in sharp contrast to the high-tech glass of the TMU campus surrounding it.
column9: The historic brick house tucked behind the modern campus.

column4: A Romanesque Revival masterpiece featuring a massive clock tower and detailed sandstone carvings.
column7: The architect secretly carved his own name into the stone eaves after being denied an official plaque.
column8: A masterclass in late 19th-century civic pride. The weight of the sandstone and the intricate carvings project the permanence of city authority.
column9: The clock tower at Queen and Bay.

column4: Housed in a former Bank of Montreal branch, this ornate building is a pinnacle of late Victorian banking architecture.
column7: The building contains one of the most beautiful stained-glass domes in Canada, located in the Esso Great Hall.
column8: The Corinthian columns and decorative stone pediments are designed to inspire awe and confidence—the architectural language of wealth and security.
column9: Look for the ornate bank building at Yonge and Front.

column4: A six-story high glass and steel pedestrian atrium known as the 'Crystal Cathedral of Commerce.'
column7: The design is meant to mimic the canopy of a forest, with white steel columns branching out into an intricate web of glass.
column8: Note how the structure manages to feel organic despite its industrial materials. It is a 'Trace' of nature inserted into the Financial District.
column9: The white forest of steel in the Financial District.

column4: An outdoor industrial gallery featuring the John Street Roundhouse, the primary repair hub for the Canadian Pacific Railway.
column7: The 'Roundhouse' was a functioning repair hub for steam locomotives, featuring a rotating turntable to move massive trains.
column8: A mechanical 'Trace' of the steam era. Walking through the trains allows the reader to physically read the industrial history of Toronto.
column9: Look for the steam engines across from the CN Tower.

column1: Paupers Pub (Former Home Bank of Canada)
column4: Originally built in 1911 as a branch for the Home Bank of Canada, this Edwardian Classical building features a striking red-brick facade.
column7: The 'snake symbol' is a Caduceus—a staff of Hermes, the patron of merchants, carved into the stone to represent prosperity and trade.
column8: Institutional branding via stone. Massive columns and a heavy entablature project immovable security in early 20th-century banking.
column9: Look for the staff of the merchants carved in stone above the patio.

column4: A majestic six-story Edwardian Classical landmark that stands as one of Toronto’s earliest purpose-built luxury apartment complexes.
column7: Designed with unique asymmetrical bay windows to give each suite a custom-designed feel, mimicking private homes.
column8: Imposing symmetry paired with creamy terracotta trim creates a high-contrast 'outline' defining the luxury character of the Annex.
column9: Look for the red brick giant with the terracotta outline.
